points.text: Collect time‑series metrics for cache hit/miss, DB query count, CPU, and latency per endpoint.
Correlate the deployment timestamp of the new feature with observed metric changes.
Inspect new query parameters: are they added to cache keys, causing key fragmentation?
Profile the request path to see if extra processing or larger result sets raise CPU and DB load.
Run EXPLAIN on the most frequent queries to detect missing indexes or full table scans.
Reproduce the issue in a staging environment with realistic traffic to confirm the hit‑rate drop.
If cache keys are too granular, consolidate parameters or hash a stable subset to improve hit rate.
Adjust cache TTL, pre‑warm hot keys, and add read‑through fallback to reduce DB reads.
Collect cache metrics (hit/miss, key patterns) before and after the change to quantify the hit‑rate drop.
Audit new query parameters: ensure they are included in cache keys consistently and deterministically.
Profile CPU usage to locate hot functions; high CPU may indicate expensive serialization or key generation.
Examine generated SQL for the new parameters; look for missing indexes or broader scans causing DB load.
Run an explain plan on representative queries to verify that index usage hasn’t regressed.
Implement cache‑key normalization (e.g., sort parameters) to increase cacheability of equivalent requests.
Consider batching similar DB reads or using read‑through cache to reduce per‑request DB round‑trips.
Add circuit‑breaker limits on DB calls to prevent overload while you investigate the root cause.
After each mitigation, re‑measure cache hit rate, DB read count, and CPU to confirm load reduction.
Check pg_stat_database.cache_hits vs cache_misses to confirm cache drop and quantify hit rate change.
Run pg_stat_statements to identify top‑CPU and top‑I/O queries after the feature launch.
EXPLAIN (ANALYZE, BUFFERS) the identified queries to see plan changes, row estimates, and buffer usage.
Compare query plans before/after adding parameters; look for generic plans replacing custom ones.
Verify that new parameter values cause index scans to become seq scans; add or adjust indexes accordingly.
Ensure statistics are up‑to‑date (ANALYZE) so the planner can choose optimal plans for varied parameters.
Check for parameter‑sniffing issues; consider using plan_cache_mode = force_custom_plan for problematic queries.
Review work_mem, effective_cache_size, and shared_buffers settings; increase if memory allows to improve caching.
Inspect connection pool size and idle connections; excessive connections can increase CPU and I/O.
If reads remain high, consider adding a materialized view or query cache for expensive aggregations.
Collect cache metrics (hit/miss, latency) and DB query logs to correlate the timing of the hit‑rate drop with new queries.
Identify the new query parameters and examine their execution plans for full scans or missing indexes.
Compare plan hashes before and after the change; look for parameter sniffing causing suboptimal plans.
Add or adjust indexes to cover the new filter columns, reducing row reads and CPU usage.
Implement query caching or materialized views for frequently accessed result sets impacted by the new parameters.
If load remains high, consider scaling read replicas or sharding to distribute the increased read traffic.
Collect time‑series metrics: cache hit/miss, DB read QPS, CPU%, latency per endpoint.
Correlate spikes: map when hit rate fell to DB read increase and CPU rise.
Identify new query parameters: check if they bypass cache keys or cause cache key explosion.
Profile request paths: find hot endpoints where cache misses dominate latency.
Examine cache eviction policy and size: ensure increased key space isn’t evicting needed entries.
Validate DB query plans for new parameters; add indexes or rewrite queries to reduce CPU.
Run controlled A/B or canary: revert cache key change or disable new params to measure impact.
Implement mitigation: broaden cache key granularity, add secondary cache, or introduce read replicas.
Re‑measure metrics after changes; ensure hit rate returns >80% and DB reads drop below baseline.
